papers.title: Low-density and high-modulus steel achieved by hypereutectic TiB2 and high Al ferrite phase
papers.paper_scope: Design of a carbon-free austenite-based low-density triplex steel containing in-situ TiB2 and δ-ferrite bands in an austenite matrix, achieving low density (6.88 g cm^-3), high Young's modulus (225 GPa), and superior strength-ductility balance via conventional steel processing, with systematic investigation of deformation mechanisms through experiments and FEM simulations.

alloys.alloys_notes: Actual measured composition of the rolled sheet is Fe-16Mn-3.9Al-5.11Ti-2.09B (wt.%). Designed to contain ~14 vol.% TiB2 in a matrix of δ-ferrite and austenite.

processes.description: Vacuum induction melting of a 50 kg ingot, homogenization at 1373 K for 2 h, hot rolling in the two-phase region in five steps with a finishing temperature of 1173 K to a 3 mm thick sheet, followed by air cooling to room temperature.
processes.processes_notes: Phase diagram calculations (Thermo-Calc, TCFE9) were used to design the composition for ~14 vol.% TiB2 in a dual-phase matrix.

processing_steps.processing_steps_notes: Five rolling steps in the two-phase region to produce a 3 mm thick sheet, finishing temperature 1173 K, followed by air cooling to room temperature.

structures.overall_structure: Heterogeneous triplex microstructure comprising a fully equiaxed-austenitic matrix embedded with elongated δ-ferrite bands and micron-sized TiB2 particles at grain/phase boundaries.

phases_present.morphology: elongated bands parallel to rolling direction, average band thickness ~4.3 μm
hexagonal prisms (primary) and irregular plate-like/needle-like debris (eutectic)

defects.dislocation_density: Initial state: δ-ferrite (1.1±0.2)×10^15 m^-2, austenite (3±0.7)×10^14 m^-2. After tensile fracture: δ-ferrite (1.7±0.7)×10^15 m^-2, austenite (2±0.3)×10^15 m^-2.

grain_structure.grain_size_distribution: Bimodal matrix: austenite equiaxed ~3.9 μm; δ-ferrite elongated ~4.4 μm with band thickness ~4.3 μm.

defect_interaction.quality_description: Dense dislocation tangles at δ-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces; sparse dislocation tangles at austenite/TiB2 interfaces. All TiB2 particles are exclusively located at grain or phase boundaries.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Local von Mises strain concentrations of ~10% at small TiB2/δ-ferrite interfaces, ~16% at austenite/TiB2 boundaries, and up to ~20% near sharp TiB2/austenite and TiB2/δ-ferrite boundaries at higher deformation; strain localizations oriented at ~45° to the loading axis.

interfaces.phase_evolution: TiB2 particles formed during liquid-phase solidification (primary and eutectic); both types underwent fragmentation during hot rolling but retained inherited morphologies. δ-ferrite formed during solidification and persisted through hot rolling and air cooling. Austenite dynamically recrystallized during hot rolling, resulting in equiaxed grains.

tensile_properties.stress_strain_curves: Engineering stress-strain curve values for present steel at discrete strains: 5% ~600 MPa, 10% ~650 MPa, 15% ~670 MPa, 20% ~660 MPa, 25% ~650 MPa, 30% ~640 MPa. See Fig. 3(a) for comparison with conventional Fe-TiB2 and IF steels.

stress_strain_distribution.distribution: μ-DIC and FEM reveal strain localizations at ~45° to loading axis; high strain (~10%) at small TiB2/δ-ferrite interfaces, elevated strain (~16%) at austenite/TiB2 boundaries, and concentrations up to ~20% near sharp TiB2/matrix boundaries at higher plastic deformation.

comparative_performance.improvement_percentage: Yield strength 22% higher (503 vs 389 MPa), UTS 30% higher (672 vs 549 MPa), uniform elongation 13% vs 11%

characterization_methods.purpose: Initial microstructure observation, damage evolution, and fractography
characterization_methods.key_findings: Triplex microstructure with TiB2 particles embedded in austenite/δ-ferrite matrix; particle sizes 2.4–35 μm; mixed fracture mode with ductile dimples, quasi-cleavage of TiB2, and particle/matrix decohesion.

characterization_methods.technique: EBSD (Oxford system on Zeiss Gemini 300, 20 kV, 120 μm aperture; TSL OIM Analysis 7)
characterization_methods.purpose: Phase mapping, grain size, KAM, and GOS analysis
characterization_methods.key_findings: Area fractions: austenite ~59%, δ-ferrite ~28%, TiB2 ~13%; austenite grain size ~3.9 μm, δ-ferrite ~4.4 μm; 90.8% austenite recrystallized (GOS<2°) vs 52.2% δ-ferrite; higher KAM (dislocation density) in δ-ferrite.

characterization_methods.technique: TEM (FEI G2 F20, 200 kV; double-jet electro-polishing with 12.5 vol.% perchloric acid and 87.5 vol.% ethanol at -25 °C, 30 V)
characterization_methods.key_findings: Dense dislocation tangles in δ-ferrite vs sparse in austenite initially; crystallographic OR at δ-ferrite/TiB2 interface; significant dislocation multiplication in both phases after tensile deformation.
Mn and Al partitioned into austenite and δ-ferrite regions respectively.

characterization_methods.purpose: Three-dimensional spatial distribution and size distribution of TiB2 particles
characterization_methods.key_findings: TiB2 particle sizes range 0.8–42.7 μm; small particles (0.8–5.0 μm) prevail in numbers, while coarse particles (10.0–42.7 μm) account for >80% of total particle area fraction.

characterization_methods.technique: Synchrotron XRD (BL02U2 SSRF, 21.7 keV, λ=0.5714 Å, beam 0.5 mm × 1 mm, reflection mode)
characterization_methods.purpose: Dislocation density measurement before and after tensile straining using modified Williamson-Hall method
characterization_methods.key_findings: Initial: austenite (3±0.7)×10^14 m^-2, δ-ferrite (1.1±0.2)×10^15 m^-2. Deformed: austenite (2±0.3)×10^15 m^-2, δ-ferrite (1.7±0.7)×10^15 m^-2.

characterization_methods.purpose: Localized strain distribution during pseudo in-situ tensile testing
characterization_methods.key_findings: Strain localizations at ~45° to loading axis; high strain (~10–20%) near TiB2/matrix interfaces; strain incompatibility between austenite and δ-ferrite with higher local strain in softer austenite.

characterization_methods.technique: Hydrostatic weighing (Archimedes' principle) with electronic densimeter
Impulse excitation technique with solid material dynamic performance analyzer
characterization_methods.key_findings: Young's modulus of 225 GPa on 100 mm × 25 mm × 4 mm specimen.

characterization_methods.technique: Quasi-static uniaxial tensile testing (SANS CMT-3000, nominal strain rate 1×10^-3 s^-1, room temperature)
characterization_methods.key_findings: Yield strength 503 MPa, UTS 672 MPa, uniform elongation 13% for dog-bone specimens with gauge length 25 mm and width 6 mm cut parallel to rolling direction.

computational_details.simulation_method: Finite element method with 2D EBSD-based RVE (200 μm × 200 μm, 500×500 CPE4 elements, 0.4 μm mesh size) and periodic boundary conditions; TiB2 treated as linearly elastic; matrix plasticity via Von Mises yield criterion with Taylor, Hall-Petch, and Kocks-Mecking hardening; damage via Weibull-distributed particle fracture and cohesive-zone interface debonding.
computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Simulated true stress-strain curves and dislocation density evolutions closely match experimental measurements; strain distributions validated against μ-DIC results.

analytical_model.equation_role: Dislocation strengthening contribution: σ_dis = α M μ b √ρ
analytical_model.parameters: α=0.2; M=3.06 (austenite) / 3.16 (ferrite); μ=65 GPa (austenite) / 80.7 GPa (ferrite); b=0.258 nm (austenite) / 0.248 nm (ferrite)
kHP=460 MPa μm^-1/2 (austenite), 130 MPa μm^-1/2 (ferrite); d=3.9 μm (austenite), 4.4 μm (ferrite)

analytical_model.equation_role: Evolution of dislocation density during straining: dρ/dε = M(k1√ρ - k2ρ)

unmapped_findings: Specific modulus (E/ρ) is 32.7 GPa g^-1 cm^3, remarkably higher than typical ~25 GPa g^-1 cm^3 for light metals such as aluminum, magnesium, and titanium.
Specific yield strength (SYS) is 73.1 MPa g^-1 cm^3 and specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) is 97.7 MPa g^-1 cm^3.

Compared to typical Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (E 160–190 GPa with 8–10 wt.% Al), the present steel achieves 18%–40% higher Young's modulus at a comparable density (~6.88 g cm^-3) with only ~4 wt.% Al.

Compared to interstitial-free (IF) steel and the conventional Fe-TiB2 reference steel (σy=389 MPa, σu=549 MPa, UE=11%): the present steel shows nearly 100% higher yield strength than monolithic ferritic Fe-TiB2 steel reported in literature (257 MPa), and 22%/30% higher yield strength/UTS than the existing Fe-TiB2 reference with improved uniform elongation (13% vs 11%).
Equilibrium phase fractions from Thermo-Calc (Fig. 1(a)): at 1050°C ferrite 0.4 + austenite 0.6 + TiB2 0.15; at 1200°C ferrite 0.85 + TiB2 0.15; at 1350°C liquid 0.4 + ferrite 0.6 + TiB2 0.15.

Damage initiation: first TiB2 particle fracture occurred at true plastic strain εp=0.0069 and applied true stress σa=556 MPa (exceeding yield stress 503 MPa). Austenite-surrounded TiB2 particles exhibited significantly higher fracture frequencies than ferrite-surrounded ones due to multi-directional shear strains from surrounding grains.

Post-fracture analysis revealed numerous voids from cracking of large primary TiB2 particles (oriented nearly perpendicular to tensile axis) and substantial particle/matrix decohesion; fracture surface shows mixed mode of ductile dimples, quasi-cleavage of TiB2, and interfacial decohesion.

FEM analysis indicates δ-ferrite provides a stable ~170 MPa strengthening contribution (>30% of overall yield strength) throughout deformation via composite effect, while austenite contributes increasing strain hardening due to rapid dislocation accumulation and geometrically necessary dislocations from phase heterogeneity.
